chunk_summary: Continued breastfeeding beyond six months, alongside complementary feeding, is recommended up to two years or longer, with gradual and family-specific weaning plans to avoid maternal and infant complications.
learning_objectives: Understand the benefits of continued breastfeeding beyond six months for both mother and child.
Learn strategies for introducing complementary feeding alongside breastfeeding.
Develop family-specific, gradual weaning plans to minimize risks such as engorgement and mastitis.
Recognize the importance of emotional support and structured plans during weaning, especially for working mothers.
key_facts: Breastfeeding should ideally continue to 2 years or longer, as mutually desired.
Complementary foods should add to, not replace, breast milk initially.
Abrupt weaning increases the risk of maternal engorgement and mastitis.
Weaning should involve dropping one feed at a time and substituting with age-appropriate foods or milk.
Frequent breastfeeding does not 'spoil' the child and provides ongoing immune protection.
Structured weaning plans are crucial for mothers returning to work.
reference_quote: "Breastfeeding should continue beyond 6 months alongside complementary feeding, ideally to 2 years or longer as mutually desired." (Source: WHO fact sheet on infant and young child feeding)
document_key_topics: Maternal and infant health during weaning
